text.title: Page Object Model and Property-Based Testing
text.summary: POM encapsulates page structure and interactions in classes so tests read as user actions and UI changes need updating in one place. Property-based testing generates hundreds of cases automatically and finds edge cases that example-based tests miss.

section.title: Page Object Model (POM)

rule.statement: Every page or major UI section gets a class with: locators defined in the constructor using data-testid, action methods that return the next page object, and no assertions inside the page class.
rule.rationale: Locators defined in one class mean a UI restructuring requires one change, not changes in every test. Returning the next page object from action methods gives tests a chainable, type-safe navigation API. Assertions belong in tests, not page objects, to keep the class reusable.

section.reference: Full Playwright POM with BasePage, LoginPage, DashboardPage

antipattern.description: Using text content or DOM structure as locators (e.g., `text=Login`, `div.form > button:first-child`).
antipattern.reason: These locators break on copy change or UI refactoring. data-testid attributes are stable by contract and immune to visual redesign.

section.title: Property-Based Testing

rule.statement: Write property tests for functions with large input spaces: round-trip properties (encode → decode → original), invariants (balance never negative), and idempotence (f(f(x)) == f(x)). Use Hypothesis (Python) or fast-check (JS).
rule.rationale: Example-based tests cover a handful of cases; property tests generate hundreds and automatically shrink failing inputs to their minimal reproducible form. This uncovers off-by-one errors and edge cases that no engineer would think to write.

section.note: Hypothesis shrinks failing cases automatically: if a test fails for a list of 47 integers, it will report the minimal failing list (often 1 or 2 elements). This is the property-based testing killer feature.
